source.title: Schizophrenia risk from complex variation of complement component 4
source.authors: Sekar A, Bialas AR, de Rivera H, et al.

AA.effect: AA carriers have increased C4A protein expression leading to excessive synaptic complement activity during postnatal development. Associated with approximately 1.3-fold increased schizophrenia risk. Elevated C4A drives excessive synaptic pruning, reducing synaptic density in key brain circuits involved in cognition and perception. May require atypical antipsychotics with different mechanisms.

AG.effect: AG carriers show intermediate C4A expression with moderate elevation in synaptic complement activity. Associated with mild increase in schizophrenia risk. Moderately elevated synaptic pruning affecting brain development and circuit formation.

GA.effect: GA carriers show intermediate C4A expression with moderate elevation in synaptic complement activity. Associated with mild increase in schizophrenia risk. Moderately elevated synaptic pruning affecting brain development and circuit formation.

GG.effect: GG carriers have normal C4A expression with baseline synaptic complement activity during development. Baseline schizophrenia risk with typical antipsychotic response patterns. Normal synaptic pruning and brain circuit development.

notes: Landmark 2016 study revealing C4A as a major schizophrenia risk locus. C4A variation drives differential synaptic pruning during postnatal brain development, with higher C4A expression increasing pruning and schizophrenia risk. This represents a fundamental shift in understanding schizophrenia genetics from purely neurochemical models to immune-developmental mechanisms.
